blurb: Wiry, branching stems carry wine-red pincushion flowers from midsummer through early fall — a pollinator magnet that weaves airy drifts through a border without ever dominating it.
water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established
seasons: Burgundy-red pincushion flowers Jul–Sep (sometimes into Oct with deadheading); dies to the ground in winter; basal rosette persists into late fall
wildlife: Excellent pollinator plant — bees, small native bees, and butterflies visit constantly; long bloom season extends nectar availability into fall
origin: Balkans and SE Europe (Macedonia and surrounding region)
habitat: Rocky meadows, open grassy slopes, and scrubby hillsides

care.hardiness: USDA zones 5–9; reliably hardy on the Front Range (5b–6a). Plants die back to a basal rosette in winter and resprout from the crown in spring. Cold-hardiness is improved by excellent drainage — wet clay in winter is more damaging than cold itself. No winter protection needed in Boulder.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in-ground in spring (late April–May) or early fall (mid-September). Spring is the safer bet on the Front Range — plants establish before summer heat. Fall planting works if done at least 6 weeks before hard freeze so roots can anchor. In containers, plant in spring; knautia in pots dries out significantly faster than in-ground and the thin-necked crown is more vulnerable to freeze-thaw heave — water more often through summer and either sink the pot into the ground or move it to an unheated garage over winter.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun is essential — 6+ hours of direct sun gives strong, upright stems and maximum bloom. In part shade, stems flop and bloom drops off sharply. The intense high-altitude sun of the Front Range suits it well as long as soil moisture is maintained through midsummer heat.[1][2]
care.soil: Well-drained, moderately fertile soil. Knautia tolerates and even prefers lean, gritty, or rocky soil; it performs well in native Front Range clay-loam provided drainage is decent. Avoid waterlogged or consistently wet ground. Near-neutral to slightly alkaline pH (6.5–7.5) suits our soils. Rich, amended soil promotes lush growth that flops; lean soil keeps it upright.[1][2]
care.water: Low to moderate — water deeply once or twice a week while establishing (first season), then reduce to deep watering every 1–2 weeks in summer. It is notably drought-tolerant once its root system is established. Avoid overhead watering in humid conditions to reduce mildew risk.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space 18–24 in apart. Plants are clump-forming and do not spread aggressively, but they need air circulation between neighbors to stay upright and mildew-free. When weaving through a border, closer spacing (15 in) encourages the airy, intermingling look the plant is prized for.[2]
care.propagation: Seed is the most reliable method: sow indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost at 65–70°F; germination takes 2–3 weeks. Seed needs no pre-treatment. Direct sowing in fall also works — cold stratification over winter improves spring germination. Division of established clumps is possible in spring (March–April) but the crown resents disturbance; lift and divide every 3–4 years to prevent central die-out, replanting vigorous outer sections. Basal stem cuttings in spring also root readily.[1][2]
care.bloom: July through September on the Front Range, with dedicated deadheading sometimes extending the display into October — still one of the longer bloom seasons of any perennial in this size class. Deadheading spent flowers encourages continuous production. A light shear in mid-July (cutting by about a third) can refresh the plant and extend the late-season display.[1][2]
care.feeding: Light feeder — a single application of balanced slow-release fertilizer (10-10-10) in early spring is plenty. Avoid high nitrogen, which causes lush, floppy growth and reduced flowering. On reasonably fertile Front Range soil, no supplemental fertilizer may be needed at all.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead or shear lightly throughout summer to maintain continuous bloom. Cut stems back by one-third in mid-July if the plant becomes leggy or sprawling. Plants have a tendency to sprawl on rich soil or in windy sites — stake with a neighbor plant or a low hoop support for the best display. Cut back to the basal rosette in late fall or early spring once frost kills the stems. Divide every 3–4 years in spring to maintain vigor.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-sows modestly — a welcome trait in a border, producing seedlings close to the parent that fill gaps and vary slightly in color (some paler pink-mauve). Thin or transplant volunteers in spring. Not aggressive; easy to manage by deadheading if unwanted.[1][2]
care.troubles: Powdery mildew can appear in late summer in humid or stagnant conditions — choose a sunny, well-ventilated spot and avoid overhead watering. Aphids occasionally cluster on new growth; knock off with water or treat with insecticidal soap. Rot in wet or compacted soil; sharp drainage prevents it. Generally a tough, low-problem plant once established.[1][2]
care.companions: Excellent paired with grasses (little bluestem, Karl Foerster feather reed grass), salvias, Russian sage, achillea, and other airy perennials that benefit from the visual thread of its wiry stems weaving through them.[1]

edible.summary: Knautia is an ornamental pincushion-flower relative with no established food use — not notably poisonous, but there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it.[2]
edible.caution: Knautia macedonica is grown strictly as an ornamental and pollinator plant. Authoritative sources record no toxicity rating and no established culinary or food use, and there is no documented safe edible preparation. With no positive food record, treat it as a non-food plant and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots. It belongs to the same family as scabious/teasel and is similarly a non-food garden plant.[2]
